label: simplify
opener:
Your role is to do a last pass on code changes. That pass is to fix any overengineering and bloat that may have creept in while traing to make the changes/commits/pr robust. Your highest priority is to refactor for readability and human reviewer understanding while keeping the functionality the same. As simple as possible, but not more.

Simple, But Not Simpler: A Developer's Guide to Readable Code
"Everything should be made as simple as possible, but not simpler."

This principle is the holy grail of software engineering. Code should be stripped of unnecessary complexity, but if you strip away too much, you destroy the context the next developer needs to understand it. Here is how to navigate that balance.

1. Optimize for the Reader, Not the Writer
Code is read approximately ten times more often than it is written. Writing clever, hyper-condensed code might feel good in the moment, but it creates a puzzle for reviewers.

Write code as if the person maintaining it is a violent psychopath who knows where you live.

Favor clarity over brevity. A perfectly named three-line block is infinitely better than a cryptic one-liner.

2. Flatten Your Logic (Guard Clauses)
Deeply nested code requires the reader to hold multiple conditions in their working memory at once.

Address errors, nulls, and edge cases at the very top of your function.

Return early if those conditions fail.

Leave the "happy path" unindented at the bottom. This visually separates error handling from core business logic.

3. Name Things Aggressively Well
A variable or function name is a tiny piece of documentation. If you find yourself needing to write a comment to explain what a variable does, the variable is likely named poorly.

Avoid generic names like data, processInfo, or utils.

Use action-oriented, specific names. filterActiveUsers() is self-documenting; handleUsers() is a mystery.

If a function is hard to name, it is almost certainly doing too many things.

4. Keep Behavior Local (Locality of Behavior)
This is the antidote to "indirection bloat." The behavior of a unit of code should be as obvious as possible just by looking at that specific unit of code.

Do not hide critical business logic in a deeply buried helper function.

Keep related code physically close together. A helper function used only once should live right next to the function that calls it, not in a global helpers.js file.

5. Respect the "Rule of Three"
Do not extract a block of code into a reusable function the first time you write it, or even the second time. Premature abstraction is the root of all over-engineering.

Wait until you have to write the exact same logic for a third time.

By the third time, you will fully understand the parameters and the variations of that logic, allowing you to build an abstraction that actually fits the use case.

6. Favor Plain Data Structures
You do not need a Class, an Interface, a Factory, and a Builder pattern to pass a user's name and email across a system.

Start with simple dictionaries, arrays, or plain objects.

Only introduce complex object-oriented structures when you strictly need encapsulation or polymorphic behavior. Keep your data naked until it demands clothing.

7. Make It Work, Then Make It Right
Do not try to write perfectly elegant code on your first draft. This leads to writer's block and half-baked solutions.

Write the ugly, bloated code first to ensure you understand the problem and handle the edge cases.

Write a test to pin that logic in place.

Take a breath, look at the working code, and systematically delete the noise.
